metadata.from: Bob (Robert E. Hanifen) — return address on envelope is PFC Robert E. Hanifen, signed 'Bobby' in letter
metadata.to: Dad (James Edward Hanifen Sr.) — letter opens 'Dear Dad'
metadata.location_written_from: Italy (likely near Caserta or a depot in southern Italy, based on references to Foggia, Bari, and Caserta)
metadata.location_sent_to: Hanifen Body & Paint, 904 Keo Way, Des Moines, Iowa
metadata.envelope_notes: 8-cent US Air Mail stamp (green, airplane design). Sender address: PFC Robert E. Hanifen-11170600, S2D. A-3, APO-19398-A, c/o Postmaster, New York, N.Y. Sent Via Air Mail / Correo Aereo. Note: postmark date (Feb 1, 1946) predates the letter's written date (Feb 28, 1946) — this may indicate the envelope was reused or there is a misdating; it is possible the postmark year is actually 1946 but the letter was written later and mailed subsequently, or there is an error in one of the dates.
topics: job change from driving wrecker to office worker
upcoming travel to Foggia and Bari
Austrian woman at Red Cross Club
people_mentioned: Austrian woman (unnamed, met at Red Cross Club)
Jim (father — note at bottom references bonds from Jim Sr.)
metadata.mood_tone: Upbeat, conversational, and curious — Bob is clearly enjoying his new office role and is engaged by the people and culture around him. Lighthearted observations about German POW workers, Italian wages, and bartering with cigarettes. Slight wonder at the post-war world around him.
metadata.legibility_notes: Typed letter, generally very legible. A few words are struck through or overtyped (e.g., 'Film' with correction). Handwritten closing note at bottom left is slightly harder to read. The word 'Miro' before 'Film' may be 'Miro' or another name — likely a film plant name. '275 lire' is split across a line as '2751ire' (typewriter artifact). 'Dego' appears to be slang for Italian language/person.
metadata.historical_context: Post-WWII occupation of Italy, February 1946. Bob is stationed with an ordnance inspection unit overseeing motor pools including a German Prison Camp. German POWs are being used as labor to maintain US military equipment. References to displaced persons (Austrian woman and her sister fleeing Yugoslavia after her brother was killed by Germans). Mentions of Italian economy — low wages (275 lire/day ≈ $1.25), barter economy with cigarettes. Plans to travel to Foggia and Bari suggest ongoing inspection duties across southern Italy.

pages.transcription:
Feb. 28, 46

Dear Dad

    Just got to work and since I dont have much to do I am going to write you a letter.  First I must tell you my job was changed from driving a wecker to the tune of an office worker in the ordinance Inspector office not a bad deal.  My boss is Lt. Davidson a former piolt a swell guy.  Our job is to pull routine inspection at this depot about the same as Uncle Warren job.  Yesterday we inspected a German Prison Camp No. 2  At this camp which is a large Mortor Pool we look at 10% of them they have about 226 trucks there God knows how many Jeeps .  This Gernan there drive and repairs this equipment if it wasn't for them I dont know what this Depot would  they do all the work there and in fact all over the place.  They are smart as hell.  This job will take over most of Italy the Lt. says we are suppose to go to Foggia and Bari in a couple of weeks  Probably will fly over he he says.  Went to Caserta last night and had a good timem  Met an Austrian woman at the Red Cross Club she couldnt speak a word of English this guy with me can understand a little Dego so we got a long all right.  She can speak five language but no English.  Her brother was killed by the german and she and her sister made a run for it  they used to live in Youslavia before the war  They have a cousin who lives in the states who sends them clothing .  Last week the Ginny stole all there stuff while they were at work  they work at the Miro Film [Film?] plant a USA project  34 bucks a month.  My Office girl get 2751ire a day about $1.25 in American money  You can anything done here for a cigarretes.  I picked a M3 knife at the arsenal the other day it has a blade of about 6iches .  I got a Hinie to sharpen it up sand the handle and polish it .  they will do it for nothing but the next you willfind them to busy  but give him a smoke and he will be your man for anything  you they salute us all the time  what a life.  But this talking both German and Italyian is quite a job , but Iam learning pretty fast though .  I will write again soon  we have to inspect another motor pool in a couple minutes  so goodby for now.

                                    Lots of Love
                                    Bobby

Jam no Bonds
from you,
Total $600 on 6.50

full_transcription:
Feb. 28, 46

Dear Dad

    Just got to work and since I dont have much to do I am going to write you a letter.  First I must tell you my job was changed from driving a wecker to the tune of an office worker in the ordinance Inspector office not a bad deal.  My boss is Lt. Davidson a former piolt a swell guy.  Our job is to pull routine inspection at this depot about the same as Uncle Warren job.  Yesterday we inspected a German Prison Camp No. 2  At this camp which is a large Mortor Pool we look at 10% of them they have about 226 trucks there God knows how many Jeeps .  This Gernan there drive and repairs this equipment if it wasn't for them I dont know what this Depot would  they do all the work there and in fact all over the place.  They are smart as hell.  This job will take over most of Italy the Lt. says we are suppose to go to Foggia and Bari in a couple of weeks  Probably will fly over he he says.  Went to Caserta last night and had a good timem  Met an Austrian woman at the Red Cross Club she couldnt speak a word of English this guy with me can understand a little Dego so we got a long all right.  She can speak five language but no English.  Her brother was killed by the german and she and her sister made a run for it  they used to live in Youslavia before the war  They have a cousin who lives in the states who sends them clothing .  Last week the Ginny stole all there stuff while they were at work  they work at the Miro Film [Film?] plant a USA project  34 bucks a month.  My Office girl get 2751ire a day about $1.25 in American money  You can anything done here for a cigarretes.  I picked a M3 knife at the arsenal the other day it has a blade of about 6iches .  I got a Hinie to sharpen it up sand the handle and polish it .  they will do it for nothing but the next you willfind them to busy  but give him a smoke and he will be your man for anything  you they salute us all the time  what a life.  But this talking both German and Italyian is quite a job , but Iam learning pretty fast though .  I will write again soon  we have to inspect another motor pool in a couple minutes  so goodby for now.

                                    Lots of Love
                                    Bobby

Jam no Bonds
from you,
Total $600 on 6.50
summary: Written February 28, 1946, from Bob Hanifen to his father in Des Moines, this letter describes Bob's new role as an office worker in the Ordnance Inspector's office in Italy, a change from driving a wrecker. He recounts inspecting German Prison Camp No. 2's motor pool, where German POWs perform most of the maintenance work, and notes upcoming inspection trips to Foggia and Bari. Bob also shares colorful observations about post-war Italian life: meeting a multilingual Austrian refugee woman at the Red Cross Club in Caserta, the low wages of Italian workers, the barter economy fueled by cigarettes, and his acquisition of an M3 knife polished by a German prisoner. The letter closes with a handwritten financial note about bonds, addressed to 'Jim' (his father, James Sr.).
